content: On the morning of Bhadra 24, I was actively deployed with police teams in sensitive areas including the residences of former President Bidhyadevi Bhandari, former Prime Minister Sher Bahadur Deuba, and Energy Minister Deepak Khadka. From around 9:30 AM the situation began to turn abnormal. A large crowd gathered at Deuba Chowk and gradually transformed into an uncontrolled, aggressive mob. He states that through a combined effort of police and locals, he was able to protect the municipality, police checkpoints, and ward offices, but full prevention was not possible as petrol bombs and flammable substances were used to commit arson and vandalism at the residences of the former President, former Prime Minister, ministers, and other senior officials.

Although the office of Police Division Budhanilkantha was not set on fire by the demonstrators' actions on that day, some motorcycles parked outside were set on fire; windows of the office and CRV vehicle were shattered by stone throwing; minor damage was caused to the building; however, weapons, case files, documents, and inventory inside the office were kept safe.

He states: 'The situation had fully transformed into a riot, the absence of strong support from other security agencies, instructions not to use lethal weapons, and the organized nature of the aggressive crowd created conditions where police alone could not bring it under control.'

He mentions hearing the instruction 'keep your weapons, communication sets, vehicles, and yourself safe; use no force other than tear gas' from Vector Control via communication set. Following the orders, he adopted one strategy after another to keep vehicles and materials safe  moving 24 vehicles to a safe area, placing all office materials into vehicles, and collectively leading police personnel toward a safe location. They left the office at around 8:00 PM and proceeded to the task force. Although the Armed Police Force was deployed, no assistance was received during the abnormal situation, and no coordination whatsoever between security agencies was possible.
67. Statement of Office Chief of Police Division Kandaghari, Police Inspector Rajan Thapa:

On Bhadra 23, during the demonstration called by the Gen-Z generation, I was deployed on duty from 8:30 AM as per the security plan on the rally route at Bijulibazar, Kathmandu. My callsign was H24. I was assigned the responsibility of diverting vehicles near the Bijulibazar bridge if necessary. The crowd coming from the Maitighar direction was of mixed character  some groups were chanting ordinary slogans while others displayed extremely agitated and aggressive behavior. As the crowd grew, stone throwing, loud slogan chanting, and aggressive activities increased. After the curfew order was issued at 12:30 PM, I repeatedly informed the crowd through public address announcements but the crowd paid no heed. As various groups merged in, the situation became further uncontrolled. In that situation, following the principle of minimum and proportionate use of force, I first attempted persuasion, public address announcements, and repeated warnings, and when those proved ineffective, conducted a baton charge, used 8 cells of tear gas, and fired 8 rounds of blank fire to stop the aggressive crowd heading toward Baneswhar.

After completing duty and returning to the office at 8:00 PM on the 23rd, he conducted a debriefing on the day's events, alerted police about potential risks the following day in his AOR (area of responsibility), and gave instructions on human rights and proportionate use of force.

On Bhadra 24 as well, I was deployed from 8:30 AM toward the west of Bijulibazar bridge. While I was on duty, demonstrators vandalized and set fire to my office, completely burning it down. 50 rounds of SLR live ammunition, 40 blank rounds, 40 rounds of .303 rifle live ammunition, 7 rounds of Type 54 pistol live ammunition, 20 rounds of shotgun rubber bullets, and 25 rounds of gas shells were destroyed, but the firearms themselves were kept safe. On that same day, I learned through the radio set that extensive arson, looting, and vandalism had occurred at the traffic office, temporary police beat at Birendra Chowk, ward offices, private residences, and government structures under my Police Division, and that arson, vandalism, and looting had also taken place in the Parliament Building area. On that day, orders came through the communication set from the Kathmandu Valley Police Office control to prepare a list of detainees and release them if such a situation arose, and to keep weapons, communication sets, vehicles, and oneself safe  however, since there were no detainees at the office, their security or transfer was not required.
68. Statement of Office Chief of Kathmandu Valley Traffic Police Office, Ram Shah Path, Senior Superintendent of Police Nawraj Adhikari:

I have been serving as Office Chief of the Kathmandu Valley Traffic Police Office, Ram Shah Path, for the past 6 months. I have read and understood the questions put to me by the honorable inquiry commission, and I provide the following clarifications regarding the above-mentioned events.

content.content: Upon receiving information that nationwide Gen-Z demonstrations and movement programs were taking place on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, and that processions and demonstrations would occur at various locations across the Kathmandu Valley, a traffic security plan was prepared to manage traffic in the Kathmandu Valley and to respond to possible incidents and damage, and all traffic police personnel were effectively mobilized. I was personally deployed in my area of direct responsibility while also providing direction and coordination.

As per orders and instructions from the supervisory office (Kathmandu Valley Police Office, Ranipokhari), 50 police personnel from this office under the command of a Police Inspector were deployed in crowd control attire as additional support at District Police Range Lalitpur. The deployed police personnel faithfully carried out their assigned responsibilities there.

A total of 156 personnel  100 traffic police personnel and 56 traffic control training trainees  were mobilized for the security of the Kathmandu Valley Traffic Police Office. Due to the shortage of crowd control equipment at this office, only 5 personnel were equipped with crowd control gear along with 2 gas guns and 2 SLR weapons, while other police personnel were also stationed. While persuading the agitated crowd that was throwing bricks and stones, we managed to hold them at the main gate of the office until approximately 16:00. Thereafter, as the growing agitated crowd began hurling petrol bombs, stones, chunks of concrete, bricks, sharp objects, and khukuris from all directions and broke through the main gate to enter and commit arson as well as looting and violent activities, repeated requests were made through communication sets to the supervisory office and nearby civil police units for additional support to protect the office, but immediate support could not be obtained at that time due to the excessive crowd and clashes occurring at other locations as well.

On Bhadra 24, 2082, at around 16:10, another large group that had returned after committing arson near Singha Durbar broke through the main gate and walls of this office from all directions and entered the office premises armed with petrol bombs, stones, chunks of concrete, bricks, sharp objects, and khukuris, committing vandalism and arson. Even as the deployed police personnel showed extreme restraint and repeatedly requested that the group not vandalize or set fire to government property, the demonstrators disregarded them and in an agitated state caused damage through vandalism and arson at the office, the government vehicles, and private vehicles within the office premises.

During the incident, when agitated demonstrators attacked from all directions using petrol bombs, stones, chunks of concrete, bricks, sharp objects, khukuris, and looted weapons, 21 police personnel deployed for office security were injured, of whom 4 were seriously injured.

After the agitated crowd entered the office premises, as a last attempt to protect the office, 7 gas shells were used following the principle of use of force for crowd control to disperse the crowd while gradually falling back in a defensive manner. When demonstrators displayed various rifles and pistols looted from police and turned violent  setting fire to vehicles within the office premises and causing highly flammable petrol and diesel to ignite  flames and smoke made it impossible to see anything. Thereafter, deadly attacks began from the demonstrators and as the situation went beyond control, prioritizing the safety of personnel, all police personnel were safely evacuated through the rear of the office via the Tukucha river route. I, Senior Superintendent of Police Nawraj Adhikari, was the last to exit as the office was fully engulfed in fire and the smoke made it impossible to see anything.

The group involved in the incident committed arson and looting of Metro Traffic FM, government and seized vehicles, personal vehicles of police personnel, and other valuable items within the office and office premises, completely destroying all items and physical structures.
Details of damage to weapons, equipment, and ammunition:

2 SLRs, 15 SLR magazines, 700 rounds of SLR ammunition, SLR blank rounds (quantity unclear), 2 shotguns, 350 shotgun live rounds, 100 rubber pellets, 9 gas guns, 218 gas shells, 7 magnums, 500 magnum rounds, 1 .38 bore revolver, 36 revolver rounds, 17 units of 7.62mm Type 54 Chinese pistol, 300 rounds, 3 units of 7.62mm Type 77 Chinese pistol, 250 rounds  all damaged.
Details of damage to vehicles and equipment:
Equipment looted: 250 batons, 100 helmets, 70 riot control gear sets, 80 body shield boards, 20 soft bulletproof jackets, 13 handcuffs.
Government four-wheeled vehicles: 19 units (including pickups, trucks, JCBs, one-ton vehicles, and rackers); motorcycles: 30 units.

content: Furthermore, regarding the private and rental four-wheeler and two-wheeler vehicles held at this office for necessary legal proceedings, 28 (twenty-eight) four-wheelers and 392 (three hundred ninety-two) two-wheelers, including motorcycles and scooters, were looted and set on fire. Additionally, unidentified vehicles (motorcycles, scooters) brought from this and other offices and parked on these premises were also subjected to arson and looting.
69. Statement of Deputy Superintendent of Police Siddharaj Neupane, Office Head of the Nepal Singhadurbar Security Battalion:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), I was deployed on duty within the Singhadurbar premises and was receiving updates via communication sets regarding the Gen-Z protest and the situation outside since morning. During the protest that started from Maitighar Mandala that day, I learned through communication sets and various media outlets that protesters had broken through the barricades in front of the Everest Hotel in New Baneshwor, entered the prohibited area, and became extremely agitated, attacking security personnel and entering the Federal Parliament building to commit vandalism and arson. Based on the security analysis that protesters might turn out in larger numbers on the 24th due to the events of the 23rd and might even enter Singhadurbar, I briefed all police personnel working in our office and deployed them on duty from early morning. From the morning, I learned through communication sets and various media that protesters had set fire to various police offices, police vehicles, government vehicles, and the houses and vehicles of various political leaders and government employees. Since the security of the outer perimeter of the Singhadurbar premises is the responsibility of the Nepal Army, a team from the Narsingh Dal Battalion of the Nepal Army was also deployed at Singhadurbar. At approximately 12:45 PM, protesters broke the security cordon in front of the Bhadrakali Temple and advanced toward the west gate of Singhadurbar. A security cordon consisting of Nepal Police (with water cannons), Armed Police Force, and Nepal Army had been established at the west gate. Compared to the Nepal Army and Armed Police, the visible protesters attempted to enter through the west gate at around 13:20 PM. At that time, some protesters appeared persuasive, while others seemed extremely angry. Although called a Gen-Z movement, the protest appeared to involve various age groups. Some protesters arrived wrapped in the Nepali flag. Some were seen wearing N95 masks, carrying water bottles, and wearing helmets. The team of Deputy Superintendent of Police Hemraj Ghimire from the National Police Training Academy, the team of Senior Superintendent of Police Rajesh Upreti from the Armed Police Force, and the team of Battalion Commander Khadka from the Narsingh Dal Battalion of the Nepal Army, who were deployed at the outer perimeter of the west gate, attempted to stop the protesters through persuasion and the use of minimum force. The crowd broke through the outer perimeter, which was even under the protection of the Nepal Army, within about 5 to 10 minutes and entered. The team of Operational Commander Deputy Superintendent of Police Sundar Tiwari from the District Police Range Kathmandu, the team of Deputy Superintendent of Police Kiranjung Kunwar from the Nepal Police Headquarters, the team of Deputy Superintendent of Police Hemraj Ghimire from the National Police Training Academy, and the police team from this Singhadurbar Security Battalion attempted to lock the gate and persuade the crowd to prevent entry, but the crowd grew more chaotic and began breaking the locks on the gate and setting the gate on fire. Immediately, my police team and teams from all security agencies dispersed the crowd that had briefly entered using tear gas and pushed them out. As the crowd grew in number and became more aggressive toward the policepelting stones and bricks and aiming iron rods, sticks, and weapons like SLRs at security personnel through the gatethey began entering through the west gate again. Despite attempts to stop the crowd with more tear gas, a large number of protesters entered through the west gate of Singhadurbar for the second time at approximately 15:00 PM. As a large number of angry protesters entered the Singhadurbar premises, presenting themselves aggressively toward the police, attempting to attack on sight, and issuing warnings, the police team, including myself, followed the suggestion and assistance of the Nepal Army's Narsingh Dal Battalion. We moved through the armory (kotghar) of the Narsingh Dal Battalion to the barracks ground located to the north. While at that ground, I saw the Kathmandu Valley Traffic Police Office on fire. At that time, I saw thousands of protesters shouting slogans and riding motorcycles and other vehicles around the Singhadurbar premises. To prevent the angry crowd from reaching them, the four-wheelers and weaponry of this Singhadurbar Security Battalion were moved to and secured at the Secretariat of the National Security Council in coordination with them. Other police personnel working at the battalion also moved to the Narsingh Dal Battalion of the Nepal Army and the National Security Council office for safety. On that day, the police team of our battalion, including myself, had dinner at the Nepal Army battalion. Later, at around 11:30 PM, while coming out of the Narsingh Dal Battalion, I saw smoke rising from fires in most ministries and offices. At that time, I saw fire engines extinguishing a fire on the west side of the Office of the Prime Minister and Council of Ministers. Afterward, upon checking the office, the outer perimeter and the office barracks were all secure. The building and barracks were safe, but communication sets had lost connection, and electricity and water were completely cut off. After inspecting the entire office, I immediately had the weaponry that had been secured at the National Security Council Secretariat brought back to our office that night. The next morning, while observing the Singhadurbar premises, I saw that the western part of the Office of the Prime Minister and Council of Ministers had sustained significant damage from arson. Similarly, vehicles parked in the front on the eastern side were completely burnt. The Ministry of Home Affairs was completely gutted by fire, and the vehicles in the front parking lot were completely burnt. The building of the Ministry of Education and the National Vigilance Center was also set on fire, and vehicles in the front and underground parking lots were completely burnt. The Traffic Police Office at Singhadurbar, the DDC, and the private and government vehicles parked in front of them were completely burnt. The Federal Parliament Secretariat and the parliamentary party offices of political parties including the Nepali Congress, CPN-UML, Rastriya Swatantra Party, and Rastriya Prajatantra Party were also completely vandalized and set on fire. Arson was committed at the Ministry of General Administration, and vehicles there were completely burnt. Ministries such as the Ministry of Law, Ministry of Land Management, Cooperatives and Poverty Alleviation, Ministry of Physical Infrastructure, Ministry of Communication, Ministry of Youth and Sports, Ministry of Tourism, and Ministry of Labour, Employment and Social Security sustained minor vandalism, while some vehicles were burnt or partially damaged. Afterward, upon observing the vehicle damage within the Singhadurbar premises, it was found that 218 four-wheelers parked in the ministry compounds were completely destroyed, 2,645 four-wheelers were partially damaged, and 57 two-wheelers were completely burnt. Later, while reviewing CCTV footage, I saw protesters who had entered through the west gate shortly afterward enter the main building of the Office of the Prime Minister and Council of Ministers from the southwest and eastern sides, take items out, and vandalize them. Afterward, I saw them enter the parking area, vandalize vehicles, and commit arson on the western side. Subsequently, protesters who entered the Ministry of Home Affairs committed arson and vandalism. Then, protesters scattered everywhere vandalized the ministry and office buildings and vehicles they encountered. In this manner, arson and vandalism were carried out on government and personal four-wheelers and two-wheelers parked in front of various ministries and offices, and most ministry offices were vandalized and set on fire. Most of the individuals committing arson and vandalism at the various ministries, offices, and vehicles were seen wearing helmets and masks, some had tied handkerchiefs over their faces, and many protesters were seen making videos with their mobiles. It was observed that fire extinguishers in the ministries were opened and all chemicals were discharged; tools such as shovels and iron rods, and whatever could be found within the Singhadurbar premises, were used for vandalism. For arson, it was observed that petrol was extracted and pieces of cloth were soaked to start fires. After 15:30 PM, the protesters destroyed and damaged the entire CCTV camera network of the Singhadurbar premises. The CCTV network, which was completely shut down from that time, became operational again from September 14 (Bhadra 29), at which time 19 CCTV cameras in the Singhadurbar premises were functional. The communication system of Singhadurbar also became fully operational only from the 29th. Similarly, at around 15:00 PM, a crowd shouting slogans and acting aggressively attempted to enter Singhadurbar through the South Gate, the main entrance for the general public. When Police Inspector Gyanendra Bahadur Khatri from this office and the security team from other agencies providing reinforcement tried to stop them, the crowd became even more chaotic, began pelting stones and committing arson, and entered the Singhadurbar premises. Outside the East Gate, approximately 2,300 protesters were burning tires and shouting slogans against the then government; Police Inspector Buddhi Prasad Subedi from this office and a team from the Nepal Army were deployed at that gate. After protesters who entered through the west gate set fire to the Ministry of Home Affairs and Ministry of Education and began advancing, the Nepal Army team and our team moved to the National Security Council Secretariat for safety. Since fire was repeatedly seen on the upper west and northwest portions of the Office of the Prime Minister and Council of Ministers for three to four days, the Nepal Army was informed and it was extinguished using fire engines. I have submitted the available CCTV footage related to the arson and vandalism incidents within the Singhadurbar premises, along with the report prepared by the Singhadurbar Security Battalion regarding the damage within the premises, with this statement. Anyone involved in planned entry into various government and non-government offices in such large numbers, carrying weapons and committing vandalism and arson, must be punished according to the law. The details written above as I have stated are true and correct. There shall be no discrepancy."
70. Statement of Deputy Superintendent of Police Chinimaya Tamang, working at the National Police Training Academy:

During the demonstrations on September 8 and 9, 2025 (Bhadra 23 and 24), I was deployed as the training coordinator for the training conducted within the office premises. On Bhadra 23, the training was conducted regularly as usual. Since training activities were ongoing within the office, there was no interest or commentary regarding the incidents in the field beyond mutual conversation. On the 24th as well, the training at the Academy was continuing. From 8:00 AM, daily office work and classes were being conducted.

Around 200 personnel from the Academy were deployed on field duty, while the remaining staff were assigned to gate duty, perimeter duty, and standby duty. Accordingly, all of us staff remained on standby. It must have been around 10:00 AM when news started appearing on online outlets about arson occurring outside. Around 80 to 90 individuals gathered near the No. 3 East Gate of the Academy and were shouting slogans. This number fluctuated between 100 to 150 and 60 to 70. During that time, sounds similar to objects exploding along with thick plumes of smoke began coming continuously from near the Maharajgunj intersection, and the individuals gathered at the aforementioned location turned toward the Maharajgunj area. However, a group of 50–70 people remained there shouting slogans. A small number of around 10–15 Nepal Police personnel in the front line and 20–25 APF personnel in the back line were containing the crowd. Until then, Nepal Army vehicles were moving back and forth between Shital Niwas and the Army Battalion located in front of the Academy. At that time, there was even an Army barricade in front of Gate No. 1, the main gate of the Academy. It must have been after approximately 15:00 PM when the Army group removed the barricade and their presence ceased. Once the Army movement stopped, the crowd at the East Gate of the Academy slowly began to advance. The police personnel deployed there also slowly retreated while the crowd grew excited and aggressive as they advanced. The size of the crowd had not increased significantly at that time. As the crowd continued to advance, many police and even Armed Police personnel who had been in the field began entering the Academy premises, so the number of police increased. But now, a kind of fear grew among us. Feeling that weapons must be protected at all costs, I, along with DSP Gunaraj Budhathoki and Inspector Amarendra Singh, became busy moving weaponry from the current quarter-guard to the barracks building, using vehicles and having staff carry them. At around 16:00 PM, the attack on the President's Office had already begun. While our office adjacent to the quarter-guard was operating, a small building near the quarter-guard caught fire. Inspector Amir Gautam and other staff tried to extinguish it, but it was not possible. Just then, Inspector Juni arrived running, calling out that everyone should gather outside the gate as there was no one on duty inside the gate. I went straight to the gate. It was a situation where no police personnel were ready to stay there. I frantically started calling everyone. There was no one except Inspector Nar Bahadur Saud, Inspector Vivek Bhattarai, Sub-Inspector Juni Rai, and one or two other staff members. We had no helmets, body protectors, or batons.

Although a large number of personnelincluding Academy staff and police and Armed Police who had been deployed outsidehad gathered in the Academy courtyard, no one was in the mindset to step forward to the gate to contain the crowd. Everyone was retreating. At that time, after attacking the President's Office outside, the crowd was intent on breaking down the gate, shouting 'This is a police office, it must be burnt,' and attacking us with stones, iron rods taken from dismantled traffic islands, and road blocks. Finally, they broke the lock of the gate and began to enter. At that time, a shout of 'Where is the gas gun? Fire!' came from within our group. Some gas was fired; the exact amount was not certain. At that time, as six or seven staff members carrying shields finally moved forward to form a barrier, two or four individuals from outside moved forward baring their chests, and one entered the courtyard and sat down. At that time, we also used baton charges. The field officer at that time was Motilal Yonjan. Subsequently, the protesters fled outside. I, along with some police personnel, pulled the iron bar from outside the gate inward and closed and locked the gate. Inspector Bed Bista also helped in that task. But since he had no abnormal security gear, I pulled him back. I then requested the retreating personnel to form a group and stay at the gate. Inspector Tilak Bam, a trainee, also assisted in that task. Later, I also requested a group of Armed Police and placed a group of nine under Tilak Bam near the wooden door to the right of the gate, and a small group of Nepal Police and Armed Police on the left side. However, the protesters did not return to attack again. I remained on gate duty; it grew dark. Around 20:00 PM, I sent those on gate duty for dinner, and after 9:00 PM, Army movement began to increase again. Although the situation remained normal, I arranged for the relief of the gate duty with some groups and returned home at around 11:30 PM. The aforementioned details are true. If proven otherwise, I shall be held accountable according to the law."
71. Statement of Deputy Superintendent of Police J.C. Shah, working at the Nepal Police Unattached Gulm, Jagannath Dewal, Kathmandu:

Before the movement started, there was no risk in the prison. No early warnings regarding prison risks were received from security or intelligence agencies, and the prison conditions remained normal. On September 9, 2025 (Bhadra 24), information was received that arson had occurred at various government, private, and police offices during the Gen-Z youth protest. Between 15:00 and 15:30, Inspector Netra Bahadur Kunwar, office head of the Nepal Police Unattached Gulm, Nakkhu, Lalitpur, contacted me to report that 400–500 protesters had gathered at the main gate shouting slogans. I immediately informed the Kathmandu Valley Police Control in Ranipokhari, as well as Senior Superintendent of Police Pravin Dhital and Deputy Inspector General Om Rana. Following this, the DIG informed me that 150 Armed Police Force personnel would be sent to Nakkhu Jail for reinforcement; however, the team did not arrive. Between 15:30 and 16:00, the Inspector informed me that protesters had vandalized and set fire to the main gate of Nakkhu Jail, facilitating the escape of prisoners. Following orders from the headquarters, the security personnel stationed there retreated to a safe location with available weaponry as they could not stop the crowd. After receiving reports that escaped prisoners from Nakkhu were heading toward Sundhara saying 'Central Jail is next,' I briefed all police personnel and directed Baikuntha Raj Regmi, the acting prison administrator, to lock the main gates of the three centersBhadra, Female, and the main government gate. We coordinated with the Nepal Army's National Service Dal nearby to secure remaining weaponry. At approximately 17:00, thousands of protesters arrived from Bhote Bahal (west), Tripureshwar (south), and Sundhara. The crowd gathered at the gate of the Kathmandu Metropolitan City City Police office to drink water and shout slogans. A segment of the crowd then moved toward Central Jail, pelting stones and damaging CCTV cameras and window panes. We retaliated and pushed the crowd back. Headquarters was immediately informed and instructed us to retaliate but not use live rounds. While we lacked sufficient riot control equipment, the Gen-Z protesters and escaped Nakkhu prisoners broke through the main gate. Inside, prisoners began shouting that 'nothing remains in this countryno constitution, no courts, no Prime Ministerall records are destroyed,' and called for the prison to be vandalized. Faced with an uncontrollable situation, prisoners and protesters broke internal doors. To stop the escape, 4–5 rounds of tear gas were fired. I informed the operations commander, SSP Deep Shamsher Rana, who stated that no further reinforcements would arrive and instructed us to secure ourselves and the weaponry if control was impossible. Out of a total of 3,878 prisoners (3,441 males and 437 females) in the Central, Bhadra, and Female jails, 3,517 (3,105 males and 412 females) escaped. Our police team managed to persuade 361 prisoners to stay. To date, 1,899 have returned, while 1,618 remain at large. During the incident, internal administration helped, and some 'Nai-kayes' (prisoner leaders) used phones to urge escaped inmates to return. For future security, the aging prison walls need reconstruction, prisoner capacity must be managed, and sufficient concertina wire and CCTV cameras are required. Additionally, prisoners should be segregated by crime type. Current challenges include the lack of X-ray machines and metal detectors at gates, and the lack of separate wards for infected, chronically ill, or elderly prisoners who, legally, should be on parole. The details provided above are true and were given freely.
72. Statement of Police Inspector Bijay Raj Timalsina, working at Police Circle Kalimati:

I serve in this circle primarily as a commander for crime investigation, complaints, and field duties. On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), I was on picket duty at the South Gate of Singhadurbar; no incidents occurred. On September 9, 2025 (Bhadra 24), I took over the first shift at 06:00. At 08:00, Control informed us that protesters were burning tires at Kalanki Chowk. Since our circle head, DSP Madhu Kumar Thapa, had been injured in the Baneshwor protest the day before, I initially led the team to Kalanki. Upon arrival, 500–600 protesters were blocking the road. As they turned violent and began vandalism, DSP Thapa arrived to take command, despite his injuries and having taken painkillers. Reinforcements arrived from the City Police (Bhrikutimandap), Balambu, and Swayambhu. By 09:00, the crowd swelled to 5,000–7,000. They attacked the Balambu police vehicle and personnel, shouting that police had killed people in Baneshwor on the 23rd and should be burned alive. Following instructions from Bhadrakali Control to use minimal force and no live rounds, we remained defensive and used megaphones to plead for peace, but the crowd ignored us. Protesters locked police personnel inside the Kalanki Police Post and the Traffic Police Post and set them on fire. To prevent personnel from being burned alive, we used tear gas and baton charges. Protesters also burned the Land Survey and Land Revenue offices. By then, the crowd exceeded 10,000, armed with petrol bombs and weapons. As reports came in of widespread arson at the President’s residence, Parliament, and Singhadurbar, we were ordered to retreat to Kalimati to secure our office and weaponry. During the retreat, thousands pelted us with stones and petrol bombs; despite requests, no reinforcements reached us. The Armed Police Force at Kalanki remained inside their vehicles and fled to their base, leaving us alone. We reached Kalimati Circle to find 4,000–5,000 protesters surrounding it. We dispersed those who had already entered and tried to secure the gate, but the situation spiraled out of control as protesters from Kalanki, Balkhu, and Kalimati converged. Protesters arriving from Balkhu on motorcycles were armed with shotguns, .303 rifles, and SLRs; some even wore police uniforms. Unable to enter the main gate, they entered adjacent houses and threw petrol bombs from rooftops, setting the front rooms and the traffic building on fire. When armed protesters entered the main gate and tried to seize police shotguns, we fired blanks in self-defense. To protect the 32 prisoners in our cells from the fire, they were released under orders with instructions to report later. Following orders from Control to evacuate if necessary, we loaded all weaponry and communication sets into a van and moved toward Bhadrakali via Teku and Sahidgate. At Bhadrakali, we used tear gas and blanks to prevent the District Police Range from being looted. At 19:30, protesters tried to ram the gate with a hijacked APF truck, but we stopped them. The attackers included drug users, transport laborers, squatters, and scrap collectors. After we evacuated Kalimati, the office was looted and burnt; all furniture, records, and personal vehicles were destroyed. A shotgun and ammunition were looted; the shotgun was later recovered, but the ammunition remains missing. Approximately 60–65 police personnel were injured. We risked our lives to save the weaponry and communication equipment.
73. Statement of Police Inspector Netra Prasad Paudel, working at the Nepal Police Health Club, Bhrikutimandap, Kathmandu:

Regarding the Gen-Z movement of September 2025, on the first day (Bhadra 23), I was assigned as assistant commander under DSP Bhaskar Khatiwada. We were initially told to take 40 personnel from APF Battalion No. 1 Naxal, but the order was briefly rescinded and then reinstated at 10:30. A team of 50 personnel took weapons from the store, but since previous teams had taken all shields, we proceeded with full gear, batons, helmets, and two communication sets. We arrived at the Parliament building at 11:30 to find it surrounded by protesters pelting stones. We were stationed at the north gate (near the water tank). Throughout the day, the situation grew critical; at 14:30, tear gas and blanks were fired at the south and east gates. Protesters blocked the Nepal Army from reaching the site. At 15:00, according to the Local Administration Act 1971, the CDO and 'Victor' gave explicit orders to fire below the knee if necessary. A curfew was eventually announced. At 17:30, the Army arrived and dispersed the crowd via megaphones. By 20:00, our team was reassigned to the Baneshwor Circle for reinforcement until 21:45. On September 9 (Bhadra 24), I was on standby at my primary office, the Nepal Police Health Club. Following reports of widespread arson at Singhadurbar and Metro Police offices, I closed the club's main gate and suspended services for safety. I coordinated with the City Police in the same perimeter, finding most personnel were on field duty or in plain clothes. I remained at the club until 21:00, when the situation normalized.
74. Statement of Senior Sub-Inspector Birendra Kumar Chaudhary, working at Police Circle Kalimati:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), I completed my regular duty without incident. On September 9 (Bhadra 24), I was on mobile duty from 08:00. Reports came of protesters burning tires at Kalanki Chowk, and I joined a team led by Inspector Bijay Raj Timalsina. Since DSP Madhu Kumar Thapa was recovering from injuries, Inspector Timalsina led us initially. Initially, 500–600 people were blocking the road. As the situation grew tense, DSP Thapa arrived despite his pain. Reinforcements came from the City Police and nearby circles. By 09:00, the crowd grew to 5,000–7,000, obstructing traffic and eventually attacking police. They vandalized the Balambu police vehicle and physically assaulted personnel. The crowd was armed and shouted slogans about burning police alive in retaliation for Baneshwor. Despite our attempts to remain calm and use megaphones, the mob locked personnel inside the Kalanki and Traffic posts and set them on fire. We used tear gas and baton charges only when personnel were at risk of being burned alive. The crowd eventually exceeded 10,000, armed with petrol bombs. After receiving orders from headquarters to prioritize the safety of personnel and weaponry due to attacks on Singhadurbar and other VIP areas, we retreated to Kalimati. On the way, thousands attacked us with stones and petrol bombs. No reinforcements arrived, and the APF personnel at Kalanki retreated to their base. We reached Kalimati to find it surrounded by 4,000–5,000 people. As thousands of protesters from Kalanki, Balkhu, and Kalimati merged, the situation became uncontrollable. Protesters from Balkhu arrived on motorcycles carrying shotguns and SLRs, some wearing police uniforms. They used adjacent rooftops to throw petrol bombs into the circle premises, setting buildings and vans on fire. When armed protesters tried to seize weapons, we fired blanks in self-defense. To save the lives of detainees from the fire, they were released. Following orders to evacuate, we loaded weapons and communication sets into a van and moved to the District Police Range in Bhadrakali. Along the way, we were constantly attacked. At Bhadrakali, we successfully defended the premises using tear gas and blanks. At 19:30, we stopped protesters from ramming the gate with a truck. The attackers seemed to be a mix of drug addicts, laborers, and squatters. Following the evacuation, Kalimati Circle was looted and completely destroyed by fire. A shotgun and ammunition were stolen; the gun has been recovered. About 60–65 personnel were injured. We followed orders to use minimal force while securing vital equipment.

The ammunition has not yet been recovered. In the incident, 60–65 police personnel from this office were injured, and some protesters were injured during baton charges. The exact number of injured protesters is unknown. We used minimal force as ordered, risking our lives to save weaponry, communication sets, and police vans. My personal vehicle and belongings, along with those of my colleagues, were destroyed by fire.
75. Statement of Senior Sub-Inspector Sharad Prasad Pandey, working at Police Circle Bouddha:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), I was on duty from 08:30 in the middle lane of the road in front of the Alpha Beta Complex in New Baneshwor, under the command of the DSP. The commander assembled all teams and briefed us regarding the duty, instructing that no one should act without orders and should avoid any disputes with anyone. Following the briefing, responsibilities were assigned and we were deployed. Iron barricades were placed at the designated location, and a banner reading 'Prohibited Area' was hung on the central barricade. After preparations, I, along with some police personnel, secured the barricades, with the shield team behind us and the weapons team in the third line formation. After some time, a large group of protesters reached the barricades, kicked and pulled at them, and engaged in various slogans and verbal abuse. Our commander used a megaphone from the beginning to persuade the group. While some segments appeared restrained, others were aggressive and unruly, and it felt as though the crowd was leaderless. Soon, protesters began throwing water bottles and stones at the police, and after a period of struggle, they broke through the prohibited area. The aggression from the protesters throughout the day was terrifying and painful. Due to the attacks, I sustained injuries to various parts of my body, including a crack in my left shoulder bone joint. I remained with the commander until that evening. Per the commander's orders, our team only used tear gas shells and rubber cartridges from shotguns for crowd control; no lethal weapons were fired, and there were no deaths or serious injuries caused by us. After the duty ended, the DSP conducted a de-briefing with the deployed team.

On September 9, 2025 (Bhadra 24), we took up duty at 08:30 in the middle lane in front of the Alpha Beta Complex under the DSP's command. Shortly after, per the commander's orders, we returned to the office with him to secure the premises. We reached Police Circle Bouddha at approximately 10:45 AM and saw protesters gathered toward Bouddha Stupa and Tusal Chowk. Under the command of the Circle Head (DSP) and Inspectors, I was stationed on the front line with the deployed team for crowd management as needed. Initially, the protesters numbered around 5,000 to 6,000, which later grew to approximately 10,000 to 12,000. At first, a thin crowd attacked the police with stones. Some locals also joined in attempting to attack the police and protect the office. Gradually, the group of protesters grew, and the situation became more aggressive and terrifying. Some individuals were seen carrying khukuris, while others carried sticks, stones, logs, and bottles filled with petrol. The DSP and Inspectors repeatedly used megaphones to request and persuade the protesters, but these requests were ignored as the attacks continued. As the office and personnel were under attack, we carried out sequential baton charges, fired tear gas, and used rubber cartridges from shotguns according to the commander's legal orders to secure the office. Every effort was made to control the continuous attacks. At approximately 14:30, I reported to the DSP and Inspectors that tear gas shells were running out and the crowd could not be contained without firing live rounds. The DSP ordered that live rounds must not be fired, stating that the office is not more important than human lives. To avoid human casualties, we decided to take the weaponry and important materials to a safe location. Carrying the weapons, we safely left for the airport through a small alley near the office at approximately 15:00. On that day as well, our team only used tear gas and rubber cartridges as per legal orders; no protesters were seriously injured, and it is true that no human casualties occurred.
76. Statement of Senior Sub-Inspector Gyan Prasad Parajuli, working at Police Circle Maharajgunj, Kathmandu:

I reported to Police Circle Maharajgunj from Tikabhairav Police Post, Lalitpur, on September 5, 2025 (Bhadra 20), and have been working there since. From 18:00 on September 7 to 06:00 on September 8 (Bhadra 23), I was deployed as a mobile officer, and I was completely off-duty and resting at my home on the 23rd. I learned about the events of the Gen-Z protest called for on the 23rd through television and mobile updates while at home; I was not in the field. On September 9 (Bhadra 24), from 06:00 to 18:00, I was again deployed as a mobile officer assigned by the office. My team consisted of one driver and three others, totaling five. My call sign was Circle Mike 111. While on duty, I had one Motorola handset, one Type 54 pistol with a magazine containing 7 rounds, one CUG mobile, and one colleague carried an SLR with 27 rounds (including 10 blanks). All police personnel were in full gear. After deployment at approximately 07:30 AM, we learned that a curfew order would be issued by the District Administration Office for the area inside Kathmandu's Ring Road effective from 08:30 AM. I informed local residents of the order using a megaphone in my assigned sector. At approximately 09:00 AM, I escorted the Chief Secretary from his private residence in Hepali Height to the Prime Minister's residence in Baluwatar and then returned to my area. Subsequently, news of arson and vandalism at Harisiddhi Police Division, Lalitpur, was received via communication set. Then, an order came from the Valley Police Control Room to merge small units and divisions into the circles. Consequently, I went to the Sukedhara, Mandikhatar, and Golfutar police beats under the Maharajgunj Circle, collected all personnel and weaponry, and brought them to the Maharajgunj Circle, establishing a base there. Following orders from the Control Room to remain secure within the office with all personnel, I stayed at the office. The Circle's DSP was in the USA for training; one Inspector was deployed with 10 personnel to the Budhanilkantha area, and another Inspector was at Shital Niwas with 10 personnel. Approximately 40 to 45 of us remained at the office. At around 11:30 AM, about 200 protesters gathered outside our office, shouting slogans and pelting us with stones. We tried to persuade the crowd, but as they grew and became uncontrollable, we repeatedly dispersed them using baton charges and tear gas.

During this time, orders were received via the communication set from the Valley Police Control Room to release all detainees from custody after recording their details, and for all police units to save themselves and their weaponry and move to a safe location (though the safe location was not specified). Following this, I felt the situation becoming abnormal. All detainees in the office cells were released. Immediately upon their release, the angry crowdincluding the released detaineesbegan targeting the office and vehicles with arson. We fired tear gas shells and blanks to protect the office (however, I did not fire any rounds from the pistol I carried). By then, some police colleagues had already left the office for safe locations. About 22 of us police personnel were at the back of the office. During that time, the mob dragged Assistant Sub-Inspector (ASI) Milan Rai and Police Constable Uttam Thapa from the gate to the road and beat them to death using iron rods and sharp weapons, leaving their bodies on the street. Afterward, as the office was set on fire and we could not escape, we went to the roof of the office. Caught between life and death, I repeatedly reported the situation to the Valley Police Control Room via communication set for rescue.

After protesters looted a police communication set and began using obscene language on it, I used my mobile numbers 9851084427 and 9851296830 to repeatedly report for rescue to the Office Head, Inspector Sudarshan Panthi, DIG Dan Bahadur Karki, and SSP Bishwa Adhikari (who did not pick up). The Inspector told me to contact the Army's DMO branch, and the DIG said he was coordinating with the Army. Until we were rescued, the office up to the third floor was completely engulfed in flames, and plumes of smoke reached the roof, making it difficult for us to breathe. After calling and pleading with the Nepal Army's DMO branch about 15 to 20 times, a Nepal Army helicopter arrived at approximately 16:30 and rescued all 22 of us from the roof in four trips. We were then taken to the Bhairavnath Battalion of the Nepal Army for safety. In the evening at the battalion, the Army brought in a body, and rumors spread in the media that it was the body of a DSP. When I and other police personnel inspected the body, it was mutilated beyond recognition. However, from a mobile phone found with the body, it was identified as the deceased ASI Milan Rai. I took custody of the body and brought it to the morgue of the Nepal Police Hospital at approximately 22:30 before returning. While I was at the Bhairavnath Battalion that night, two police personnel who had been with me called to say they were hiding with weapons in the Chinese Cultural Center building and needed rescue. I informed the Control branch with their contact numbers. Rescue was not possible during the night, so at 06:00 the next morning, I reported again to SSP Deep Shamsher J.B.R., and they were safely rescued with their weapons at around 08:00 AM. The next day (the 25th), we were not sought out by our office; the Nepal Army brought us to the Police Headquarters in Naxal at around 17:00 and handed us over. Afterward, I handed over my assigned weapon, ammunition, and communication set to the logistics and communication branch and went to the Nepal Police Hospital for treatment. I was discharged the same day after a general check-up and went home.

Even though I kept myself and my subordinates safe, protected the weaponry, and ensured the safe rescue of other police personnel at risk during the incident, I feel that my current and departmental commanders have not even questioned me or shown interest to this day. I feel that my competence and work have not been evaluated.
77. Statement of Senior Sub-Inspector Omkar Chand, working at Police Circle Thankot:

On September 8 and 9, 2025 (Bhadra 23 and 24), I was deployed for standby security at my office, Police Circle Thankot, where I serve as the Subedar Major. On the 23rd, I was part of a 25-member team under Inspector Kiran Sapkota. Following instructions from the Valley Police Control to remain on high alert due to the Gen-Z protests starting at Maitighar Mandala, we remained stationed at the office. We monitored reports via radio sets about the crowd advancing toward the Parliament building, breaking barricades near Everest Hotel, scaling the main gates, and the subsequent use of force, including gunfire, by police. While these events unfolded elsewhere, no untoward incidents occurred within the Thankot Circle area that day. On the 24th, I was part of a 35-member standby team under Circle Head DSP Eliza Giri. Two other teams led by Inspectors were deployed outside at Kalanki and Baan Bhanjyang. From 10:00 AM, a mixed group of 2,000 to 2,500 protesters gathered on the main road in front of our office gate, obstructing traffic and burning tires at various intersections from Nagdhunga to Kalanki. By 11:00 AM, the crowd began pelting the office with stones and plastic bottles filled with liquid. The teams deployed at Kalanki and Baan Bhanjyang were unable to return to the office due to the chaos. By early afternoon, we received reports over the radio and saw social media footage of arson at Singhadurbar, the Supreme Court, and various police units, including reports of looted weaponry and killed personnel. Between 14:00 and 15:00, the crowd surrounding our office grew aggressive, lobbing petrol bombs and pelting stones from the front road and over the back boundary walls of neighboring houses. We focused on securing the office, weaponry, and detainees. Per orders from the Control Room to contain the situation without using gunfire, and to prioritize the safety of personnel, weapons, and communication sets while releasing detainees, we attempted to persuade the mob. However, by 16:30, approximately 5,000 people had gathered. Unruly individuals armed with iron rods and khukuris broke the main gate, set fires, and began looting the premises after the detainees were released. We loaded the weaponry, ammunition, and communication sets into a one-ton police van. As we moved toward the nearby Nepal Army barracks for safety, protesters pelted the vehicle with stones, causing damage and minor injuries to personnel. By approximately 17:00, the Thankot office was completely engulfed in flames.
78. Statement of Senior Sub-Inspector Rajkumar K.C., working at the Federal Parliament Security Base, New Baneshwor:

I served as the Subedar Major among 96 police personnel under an Inspector assigned to Parliament security. On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), a National Assembly meeting was scheduled. We were aware of the Gen-Z protest at Maitighar Mandala and were monitoring the situation. Around 11:30 AM, protesters targeted the Parliament building from all sides. While checking the duty posts at Gate No. 1, I saw a crowd of 4,000 to 5,000 on the road. They were using slingshots and pelting stones, damaging the boundary walls. I was struck in the left eye by a stone, causing severe bleeding. Due to the severity of the injury, I was taken to the medical room inside the Parliament building and then transferred to Civil Hospital, New Baneshwor. Doctors there stated I needed surgery and referred me to Tilganga Eye Hospital. I returned to the office after 17:00; I heard the sound of gunfire while at the hospital but had no knowledge of the specific damages or casualties at that time. On the 24th, I went to Tilganga Eye Hospital at 07:00 for surgery and was occupied with medical procedures throughout the day. I was unable even to use my mobile phone due to my eye condition. That evening, at 18:00, I learned that our entire unit from the Parliament Security Base had merged with the Armed Police Force office at the Airport. I later learned that some protesters died on the 23rd, police personnel died on the 24th, and major buildings like the Parliament, Singhadurbar, and Shital Niwas had been burned. Since then, our unit has been stationed at the Nepal Police Special Task Force in Kapan. My eye has not yet fully recovered, and I remain under medical follow-up. The protesters burned down the entire barracks at New Baneshwor, destroying all personal belongings, uniforms, and vehicles of the personnel.
79. Statement of Senior Sub-Inspector Deep Bahadur Rana, working at Police Circle Durbar Marg:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), I was serving as the Subedar Major at Police Circle Durbar Marg. I was part of a 40-member office security team under DSP Raj Kumar Shrestha. After receiving high-alert instructions from the Control Room regarding the Gen-Z protests at Maitighar, we remained prepared at the circle. We monitored reports via radio about the crowd breaking barricades at the Parliament building and the subsequent police firing. Per the DSP's orders, we remained on high alert throughout the day; no untoward incidents occurred at our office on the 23rd.

On September 9 (Bhadra 24), I was on duty from 06:00 with a 50-member security team. Picket duties were stationed at Keshar Mahal, Jamal, and Shanti Vatika, with CRV bases at Mahendra Salik and Lazimpat. While monitoring the radio, I heard about attacks on the Kalanki Division and other units across the valley. As the attacks escalated, the DSP ordered all external picket and mobile teams back to the office for consolidated defense. At approximately 14:00, around 200 protesters on motorcycles and on foot arrived at Keshar Mahal and began chanting slogans. Despite the DSP's efforts to cordon the area, the crowd grew rapidly. We learned via radio that the Valley Police Office in Ranipokhari was under attack. By 16:00, the group in front of our office swelled to 2,000–3,000. They began pelting stones and bottles. Despite the DSP's repeated pleas for restraint, the protesters became more agitated and attempted arson. Since we had strict orders not to fire and to prioritize the safety of our weaponry, we used only verbal persuasion and avoided further force initially. However, the mob broke the gate, entered the premises, vandalized the Duty Officer's room, and began breaking into the detention cells. Detainees who had been sent from the Cyber Bureau in Bhotahity escaped. We subsequently retreated to the nearby Narayanhiti Gulm for safety, taking the office vehicles, weaponry, and most handheld sets with us. While we saved almost all vehicles and the majority of ammunition, a subsequent inventory revealed that some scattered weapons and ammunition had been looted. We later found that our office equipment, case files, uniforms, and personal belongings were either looted or burned. When we returned at 20:00 after the crowd dispersed, the office was uninhabitable. We spent that night inside the Narayanhiti premises with the Army. Throughout the 23rd and 24th, we only used verbal persuasion and baton charges; no other force was used, though the DSP and several personnel sustained minor injuries.
80. Statement of Senior Sub-Inspector Randhir Jaisi, working at the Crime Investigation Branch, Police Circle Balaju:

I have been working in the Crime Investigation Branch of Police Circle Balaju since June 2025. On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), I was busy with internal administrative work within the branch. I had heard that the Gen-Z group was protesting in the Maitighar Mandala area regarding national interest, good governance, and anti-corruption. Per the security plan, a standby team was stationed at the circle while others were deployed for external duties. Later that day, I learned through social media that the situation at the Everest Hotel had turned uncontrollable, with protesters breaking barricades, entering prohibited areas, and vandalizing the Federal Parliament building. I also heard that several protesters died when police opened fire to stop the destruction, though I did not know the exact casualty count.

On the morning of September 9 (Bhadra 24), we were ordered to remain on standby in full uniform (Chusta). At around 07:00–08:00 AM, a team of 15–16 personnel, including myself and the Subedar Major, was securing the office. A crowd of 2,000 to 3,000 gathered at the gate, burning tires and chanting slogans. As the mob grew more aggressive, throwing stones and bottles, Inspector Dilip Kumar Humagain’s team arrived to assist. Protesters began throwing petrol bombs and using long sticks with flaming ends to ignite the office from over the boundary walls. We repeatedly extinguished fires using jars of water and fire extinguishers. When the crowd became chaotic, we used tear gas to disperse them and fired blanks as warning shots, as there were no orders to use lethal force. Eventually, the Circle Head's team returned from the field. I learned via social media that the President's residence and other private homes were also being attacked. During the struggle, a stone struck my leg, causing injury. The mob eventually set fire to the second floor of the barracks and the detention cells through the ventilators. Per orders received via radio to prioritize the safety of personnel and weaponry and to release detainees, we released 69 detainees whose remand had been extended and 5 others. To avoid a massive loss of life that would have resulted from final defensive force, we took as much weaponry and equipment as possible and retreated. Using blanks and tear gas to clear a path, we safely escorted our injured Circle Head and the team to the Nepal Army barracks in Nepaltar. I later learned through social media that two protesters were injured at Balaju and subsequently died during treatment.
81. Statement of Senior Sub-Inspector Jai Bahadur Singh, working at Police Circle Maharajgunj:

As the Subedar Major at Police Circle Maharajgunj, I am responsible for the deployment of personnel from Junior Police Officers downward. On September 8 and 9 (Bhadra 23–24), I remained at the office. On the 23rd, I learned through media that some protesters had died during the Gen-Z demonstrations. At that time, our Circle Head (DSP) was in the USA for departmental training. On the morning of the 24th, two of our Inspectors were deployed elsewhere by the District Police Range. Following reports that the Harisiddhi Police Division and other units were being burned, we merged smaller units into the circle, bringing our strength to about 40–45 personnel. Around 11:30 AM, a crowd of over 800 protesters began pelting the office with stones and petrol bombs, vowing to take revenge for the previous day's events. We heard reports that Balaju Circle, New Bus Park, and Tilingetar divisions had been burned and the mob was heading toward us. With no reinforcements available, we used tear gas and batons. By early afternoon, a massive influx of protesters from Balaju and the Bus Park arrived. Many police personnel were injured, and the mob breached the perimeter. While some of us tried to contain the fire, others tried to hold the gate. Per orders from 'Victor Control' to release detainees and secure weaponry and personnel, we attempted to evacuate. However, the fire spread rapidly, trapping some personnel who were forced to retreat to the roof, from where they were later rescued by a Nepal Army helicopter. Tragically, ASI Milan Rai and Constable Uttam Thapa were brutally murdered by the mob. Twenty-one personnel were seriously injured, and the office records and weaponry were either destroyed or looted.
82. Statement of Senior Sub-Inspector Shankar Prasad Chidi Magar, working at the Pashupati Temple Security Post:

Our primary duty is the security of the Pashupati Temple, while the outer perimeter is managed by the Armed Police Force (APF). No personnel from our post were deployed elsewhere on September 8 (Bhadra 23). On the 24th, following widespread reports of arson at Parliament, Singhadurbar, and private residences, we coordinated with the APF to secure the temple. While massive protests were visible in the surrounding area, we remained stationed within the temple premises. Protesters eventually set fire to the Pashupati Area Development Trust office near Gaushala. In the parking lot of that office, a motorcycle belonging to Police Constable Suraj Saud and two belonging to APF personnel were burned. No other loss of life or property occurred at our post; the personnel, weaponry, and documents of the Pashupati Temple Security Post remain safe.
83. Statement of Sub-Inspector Him Bahadur B.K., working at Police Circle Maharajgunj:
